title: What Home Care Aides Can (and Can't) Do
summary: Home care aides provide valuable support, but their scope is more limited than many families expect — knowing the boundaries avoids frustration later.
category: Life Transitions I: The Hospital-to-Home Arc
journeyStage: Life Transitions I: The Hospital-to-Home Arc
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: What Home Care Aides Can (and Can't) Do | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Home care aides provide valuable support, but their scope is more limited than many families expect — knowing the boundaries avoids frustration later.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Non-medical home care aides typically help with daily tasks, not medical care.
Medication reminders are usually allowed; administering medication often isn't, depending on state rules.
Skilled medical needs usually require a home health nurse instead.

paragraphs: Non-medical home care aides generally help with things like bathing, dressing, light housekeeping, meal preparation, and companionship — genuinely valuable, meaningful support, but distinct from medical care.

Rules vary by state, but many non-medical aides can remind someone to take medication and even hand them the pre-organized dose, without being permitted to administer injections or manage complex medical tasks — those typically require a home health nurse, a different (and differently licensed) type of care.

nextSteps: Ask the agency directly what their aides are and aren't permitted to do in your state.
Clarify whether medical needs require a separate home health service.
Put expectations in writing with the agency to avoid confusion later.
disclaimer: General guidance — specific rules vary by state and agency; confirm directly with your provider.
